coredata.dc:title: Strongly Focused HIFU Transducers With Simultaneous Optical Observation for Treatment of Skin at 20 MHz

coredata.dc:creator: Zawada, Tomasz
Bove, Torsten
coredata.dc:description: High-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) transducers are proposed as a new treatment modality for dermatology. The shape and size of pressure fields generated by strongly focused transducers with an f-number equal to 0.75 operating at frequencies up to 20 MHz are analyzed analytically using the Lucas–Muir model and numerically with the wide-angle Khokhlov–Zabolotskaya–Kuznetsov method. The modeling results under quasi-linear conditions are compared against measurements performed in an acoustic tank with the aid of a fiberoptic hydrophone. The size of the focal zone expressed by their depth of focus and focal diameter is found to be directly controlled by their operating frequency and f-number. Devices manufactured for an operating frequency of 20 MHz are characterized by their 6 dB depth of focus of 490 μm and focal diameter of 80.6 µm. The devices are further studied at high power levels using a polyacrylic tissue-mimicking phantom. The devices are equipped with an optical observation system allowing simultaneous treatment and observation of the skin surface. In comparison to conventional cosmetic applications of HIFU, the devices analyzed are concluded to be ideal for treatment of precisely selected and confined layers of the human skin.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: General structure of transducer with selected dimensions (in millimeters) and main components. NPD = nominal penetration depth.
Schematic of high-intensity focused ultrasound transducer integrated with optical observation system. The optical axis of the lenses is coaxial with the main axis of the acoustic field.
Setup for measurement of pressure field comprising an acoustic tank, XYZ stage and fiberoptic hydrophone. TMP = tissue-mimicking phantom.

(A) Photograph of the setup for measurement of temperature in tissue-mimicking gel, comprising an XYZ stage and a TMP holder. (B) Schematic of the TMP gel and location of thermocouple in relation to the acoustic field. TMP = tissue-mimicking phantom.
Schematic of the focusing bowl parameters used in analytical modeling. The origin (0,0) is located at the apex of the spherical radiator.

Parameters of the model as well as the computational domain used in wide-angle Khokhlov–Zabolotskaya–Kuznetsov (WAKZK) numerical modeling of transducer T20B coupled through water to TMP gel (dimensions in millimeters). NPD = nominal penetration depth; TMP = tissue-mimicking phantom.

Comparison of pressure distribution with measured DoF and FD, transducer at z = 0. (A) Along axial distance at 3.95 MHz. (B) Along radial distance at z = 15 mm at 3.95 MHz. (C) Along axial distance at 6.89 MHz. (D) Along radial distance at z = 15 mm at 6.89 MHz. (E) Along axial distance at 19.68 MHz. (F) Along radial distance at z = 15 mm at 19.68 MHz. DoF = depth of focus; FD = focal diameter; WAKZK = wide-angle Khokhlov–Zabolotskaya–Kuznetsov method.

Measurements of temperature in the focal point located in a tissue-mimicking phantom in response to an acoustic field at different levels of acoustic power: (A) burst duration of 200 ms and (B) burst duration of 500 ms. Shaded areas around measurements indicate measurement error. Black lines indicate results of WAKZK and bio-heat transfer modeling, with the spatial average around the maximum temperature point taken with a diameter of 100 μm. WAKZK = wide-angle Khokhlov–Zabolotskaya–Kuznetsov method.

Results of numerical modeling using the wide-angle Khokhlov–Zabolotskaya–Kuznetsov method for transducer T20B operating at 20.186 MHz, with geometry and computation domain according to Figure 6 at acoustic power P a = 0.5 W. (A) Negative peak pressure distribution. (B) Positive peak pressure distribution. (C) Magnitude of acoustic intensity distribution. (D) Heat density distribution. TMP = tissue-mimicking phantom.

(A) Development of spatial peak temperature and spatial average temperature over the burst time for a transducer with geometry described in Figure 6 at P a = 0.5 W. (B) Distribution of temperature at the end of the burst at t = 500 ms. Black circle indicates spatial avearging sphere of diameter 100 μm corresponding to the volume of active element of the thermocouple used in measurements. TMP = tissue-mimicking phantom.
Temperature distribution at selected time points for transducer T20B operating at 20.186 MHz, P a = 0.5 W, over the course of a 500-ms burst.

High-intensity focused ultrasound lesions created in tissue-mimicking phantom gel at 2.6 W, 1.3 J of acoustic energy, using transducers working at around 20 MHz. (A) Transducer T20D, NPD = 1.3 mm. (B) Transducer T20C, NPD = 1.8 mm. (C) Transducer T20B, NPD = 2.3 mm. NPD = nominal penetration depth.

Examples of the optical observation capability of the described transducer design with optical imaging capability. Images are taken by the integrated camera, with an optical path going through ultrasonic gel, acoustic window and approximately 13 mm of coupling medium (water). (A) Observation area with approximately 8-mm diameter. (B) Image of circles of different size; numbers are given in micrometers. (C) Image of human skin with Miescher nevus as seen through the integrated optical observation system.

Plot of DoF coefficients in a broad range of f-numbers at 20 MHz (blue lines). Ratio of location of the maximum pressure to focal length and change of pressure at focal length related to maximum pressure on main axis of the transducer (orange lines). DoF = depth of focus.
Size of 6 dB focal zone in water expressed by DoF and FD in a broad range of frequencies for selected f-numbers: a = 10 mm, F = 15 mm. DoF = depth of focus; FD = focal diameter.
Focal zone reconstructed from planar scans of pressure field at 20 MHz. Origin at maximum pressure location. Red arrow indicates the direction of acoustic radiation.

Distribution of normalized pressure as a function of the ratio of inner radius to outer radius of a transducer operating at 20 MHz along the (A) axial direction and (B) radial direction at z = 15 mm, a = 10 mm, F = 15 mm.
Temporal waveform of pressure at z = 15 mm at different levels of input acoustic power.

head.ce:title: Strongly Focused HIFU Transducers With Simultaneous Optical Observation for Treatment of Skin at 20 MHz

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Address correspondence to: Tomasz Zawada, TOOsonix A/S, Agern Alle 1, 2970 Hørsholm, Denmark.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) transducers are proposed as a new treatment modality for dermatology. The shape and size of pressure fields generated by strongly focused transducers with an f-number equal to 0.75 operating at frequencies up to 20 MHz are analyzed analytically using the Lucas–Muir model and numerically with the wide-angle Khokhlov–Zabolotskaya–Kuznetsov method. The modeling results under quasi-linear conditions are compared against measurements performed in an acoustic tank with the aid of a fiberoptic hydrophone. The size of the focal zone expressed by their depth of focus and focal diameter is found to be directly controlled by their operating frequency and f-number. Devices manufactured for an operating frequency of 20 MHz are characterized by their 6 dB depth of focus of 490 μm and focal diameter of 80.6 µm. The devices are further studied at high power levels using a polyacrylic tissue-mimicking phantom. The devices are equipped with an optical observation system allowing simultaneous treatment and observation of the skin surface. In comparison to conventional cosmetic applications of HIFU, the devices analyzed are concluded to be ideal for treatment of precisely selected and confined layers of the human skin.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, high-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) (ter Haar and Coussios 2007) has become well established as a therapeutic method for treatment of numerous diseases, with more than 150 indications reported to date (White et al. 2021). In most cases, the treatment zone is located deep in the human body as removals of tumors in brain (Lamsam et al. 2018), prostate (Guillaumier et al. 2018), thyroid glands (Lang and Wu 2017) and uterine fibroids (Quinn and Gedroyc 2015) have been the major areas of interest (Elhelf et al. 2018). Therefore, HIFU is usually accompanied by an imaging method enabling observation as well as targeting of the pathological tissue to be treated. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) (Ellens and Partanen 2017) and ultrasonic imaging are by far the most popular modalities used in experimental treatment, as well as in commercial systems. The cost and complexity of HIFU systems, and therefore their ubiquity, are greatly affected by the need for complex imaging systems, especially in the case of MRI.

Recently, the authors proposed a treatment method specifically designed for treatment of human skin using HIFU as the therapeutic modality combined with optical imaging (Zawada and Bove 2017; Bove et al. 2019). Human skin is usually treated using cryotherapy or laser therapy. Cryotherapy is by far the cheapest and the most ubiquitous; however, it does not allow for good control of the depth of treatment. Because of the surface temperature distribution, the center of the treated area is usually overtreated while the peripheral areas are undertreated, which can greatly increase the probability of scarring (Papadopoulos et al. 2020). Similarly, lasers in general require good absorption of light, which is not the case in many diseases. Moreover, the depth of treatment strongly depends on the local emissivity that can lead to local overexposures, again leading to increased probability of scarring. Probability of infection is also increased as the laser therapy is basically exposing an open wound to the outside environment (Nouri 2018).

The proposed HIFU treatment for human skin bears hope for more targeted treatment where the energy is deposited at the exact level within the dermis or epidermis, creating flat and more controllable treatment areas (Serup et al. 2020a, 2020b; Soegaard et al. 2020). In addition, the surface of the skin is intact as the point of treatment is located beneath it because of the focused nature of the ultrasonic wave. This aids the healing process as well as reduces the probability of infections. A wide range of skin conditions can be addressed with the proposed treatment, beginning with cancer conditions such as basal cell carcinoma and Kaposi's sarcoma, pre-cancer conditions such as actinic keratosis, and other conditions including seborrheic keratosis and sebaceous hyperplasia, ending with milder conditions such as vascular malformations and viral warts.

High-intensity focused ultrasound transducers for cosmetic treatment of skin (deep dermis and below) operating at frequencies up to 4–10 MHz with built-in ultrasonic imaging have been reported previously (e.g., Park et al. 2017). This frequency range, however, is not appropriate for precise treatment of shallower skin layers including epidermis, as the typical thickness of human skin is 1–2 mm. Moreover, ultrasound imaging guidance is less relevant as features located on the skin surface offer better guidance for treatment. The objective of this work was to describe HIFU transducers, including their acoustic properties, designed for dermatology applications operating at 20 MHz with integrated optical observation. It is argued that a very well defined and small focal zone created by the properly designed transducer is the prerequisite for appropriate treatment of the skin.

First, strongly focused transducers (Kossoff 1979; Goldstein 2006) operating at approximately 4, 7 and 20 MHz are studied using analytical modeling with the assumption of quasi-linear conditions and lossless medium. Special attention is paid to the relationship between the operating frequency and the dimensions of the focal zone defined by the depth of focus (DoF) and focal diameter (FD). Effective parameters of the transducers are then estimated by fitting the models with experimental data obtained in an acoustic tank using a fiberoptic hydrophone. The effective parameters are then used in numerical modeling of the transducers, operating at 20 MHz in lossy medium at high power levels exceeding quasi-linear conditions, coupled to tissue-mimicking phantoms (TMPs). To that end, this approach is similar to one described in Canney et al. (2008). Afterward, temperature rise in the focal zone of the acoustic field located underneath the surface of the TMP at a penetration depth comparable to the location of human dermis is studied experimentally using a temperature microprobe. The authors propose a method of multiparameter identification of properties of TMPs by minimizing a goal function being the mean square root difference between the temporal temperature measurement and the numerical results. The identified parameters are then used to further study lesioning using numerical modeling of pressure distribution, as well as the temperature buildup in the focal zone of the TMP at higher power levels.

A family of transducers of similar dimensions but different resonance frequency of approximately 4, 7 and 20 MHz was fabricated to allow for experimental verification and comparison of several acoustic properties of the focal zone. Transducers were housed in 3-D–printed housing made of polyamide with the dimensions indicated in Figure 1 . The housing was designed to allow for a very precise location of the focal zone with respect to the transducer housing's reference plane. The offset between the reference plane and the focus of the transducer defines nominal penetration depth (NPD), as depicted in Figure 1. The transducer housing was designed with an integrated water chamber and an opening that allowed for easy water filling, providing a perfect coupling medium when covered by acoustically and optically transparent film: low-density polyethylene (LDPE). A detailed structure of the transducer housing is provided in Figure 1.

Transducers operating at 20 MHz were fabricated with NPD as outlined in Table 1 . The choice of such nominal penetration depths corresponds to the foreseen application in dermatology, allowing for precise treatment of selected layers within human epidermis (NPD = 0.8 mm) and dermis (NPD = 1.3–2.3 mm). An NPD of 2.7 mm would correspond to treatment of subcutaneous layers more relevant for cosmetic applications, and it has been included in the study for more practical reasons, as it enables measurements of larger areas of the acoustic field in the pre-focal zone.

A piezoelectric component in the shape of a spherical focusing bowl of outer diameter (OD) 20 mm and radius of curvature (RoC) 15 mm was the core component of each fabricated transducer, with a hole of 5-mm internal diameter (ID) in the center. The piezoelectric components were manufactured from hard PZT Navy Type 4 (Meggitt, Kvistgaard, Denmark) and electroded using thick film silver. The thickness of each piezo component was designed to match a fundamental resonance frequency of around 4 and 7 MHz. The transducers operating at approximately 20 MHz were built using a piezoelectric component of fundamental frequency 7 MHz electrically matched at its third harmonic resonance. A detailed list of fabricated transducers together with operating parameters used in the presented experiments is provided in Table 1.

A round printed circuit board with electrical matching network circuit, as well as electrical connections for LED lighting, was placed on the top of the housing. Each device was terminated and electrically matched to nominal 50-Ω input impedance.

In all experiments described, the coupling medium chamber of the transducers was first filled with de-ionized water (conductivity <10 µS/cm) and then covered with 10-μm-thick low-density polyethylene film (LDPE) to prevent the water from escaping when transducers were used in the face-down position. The film provided good acoustic as well as optical transparency.

Transducers T04A, T07A and T20A were used in measurements of acoustic field in water using an acoustic tank and fiberoptic hydrophone. These transducers did not have the optical observation capability; however, the central hole was still present (blocked by 3-D–printed plastic piece for water tightness), and their nominal penetration depth of 2.7 mm allowed for measurements of the acoustic field in a larger extent in comparison to the others as the motion of the hydrophone was blocked by the LDPE film at a distance from the focal point equal to NPD. Transducer T20B was used in the measurements of transient temperature in tissue-mimicking phantoms; moreover, together with T20C and T20D, it was also used in imaging of lesions in TMPs. T20B, T20C and T20D had optical observation capability.

The optical system was fully integrated into HIFU transducers T20B, T20C and T20D by aligning the main acoustic field axis with the optical axis. Furthermore, the optical focus was adjusted so it was located on the reference plane, while the focus of the acoustic field was located below the reference plane at the distance defined by the NPD. The schematic arrangement of the setup is illustrated in Figure 2 . The imaging sensor used in experiments was a standard 2-megapixel USB 2.0 webcam equipped with a microscopic lens.

An egg white–based tissue-mimicking phantom gel was used in testing of the distribution and location of the acoustic field around the focal point. Because of the content of egg white protein, the TMP gel exposed to increased temperature transitions from a semitransparent yellow color to opaque white. The transition temperature was measured to be 60°C–65°C by submersing TMP blocks into water of increasing temperature. Two-deciliter portions were made according to the following recipe: fresh stock solutions of 10 wt% ammonium peroxodisulfate (VWR International, Søborg, Denmark) in de-ionized water were made. Commercially available pasteurized egg white was acquired fresh from local supermarkets for each batch. The egg white was thoroughly stirred, and 120.0 g was weighed into a beaker. A total of 30.0 g of acrylamide 40% solution–BIS 19:1 (VWR International) and 30.0 g of undiluted pH 9.0 10 × TRIS buffer (VWR, Denmark) were added. Thirty drops, corresponding to approximately 0.9 g, of tetramethylethylenediamine (VWR, Denmark) was added under gentle stirring. To initiate the gel reaction, 5.2 g of the ammonium peroxodisulfate stock solution was added under gentle stirring. The solution was immediately poured into 3-D–printed molds, mounted with two 76 × 26-mm2 Menzel glass plates (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) and left at 100% relative humidity overnight for gel formation. Gentle removal of the TMP gel from the molds resulted in a 40 × 26 × 10-mm3 bubble-free block of gel with very smooth and plane parallel surfaces.

The mass density of TMP gel was experimentally determined using Archimedes’ principle, with five samples of TMP gel blocks at ambient temperature equal 23°C. A microbalance (Mettler-Toledo ML204T, Mettler-Toledo GmbH, Grefensee, Switzerland) was used in the measurements with a dedicated suspension system. Average mass density was equal to 1.074 g/cm3 with a standard deviation of 0.004 g/cm3.

Acoustic field measurements were performed using an acoustic scanning tank equipped with an XYZ stage UMS3 (Precision Acoustics, Dorset, UK) combined with a fiberoptic hydrophone (Zhou et al. 2006; Morris et al. 2009) with the appropriate interrogator (Precision Acoustics). The fiberoptic hydrophone was selected for the measurements because of its exceptionally broadband response and the extremely small diameter of the active surface of approximately 10 μm, as well as the ability to handle relatively high pressure levels, making it well suited for measurement of acoustic fields from high-intensity focused transducers. The hydrophone was calibrated in the range 1–40 MHz, allowing for reliable measurement of pressure in a wide-frequency spectrum. The data acquisition system consisted of an oscilloscope (DSOX3024T, Keysight Technologies, Santa Rosa, CA, USA) integrated with the UMS3 system. The end-of-cable temporal voltage acquired in response to input pressure was converted into a temporal pressure measurement using the inverse Fourier transform of the ratio between the Fourier transform of temporal voltage response and the end-of-cable sensitivity of the hydrophone.

The transducers were driven by an in-house built broadband class-AB power amplifier of maximum output 90 W and gain 50 dB. The amplifier had flat frequency response (better than 3 dB flatness) from 1–30 MHz, allowing for driving of transducers at 4, 7 and 20 MHz. Additionally, the power driving signal path included a matched directional coupler (DDS-1, Stenbock Enterprise LLC, Aurora, OR, USA) for precise measurement of forward as well as reflected electric power. The power amplifier was driven by a signal generator 33600A series (Keysight Technologies) controlled by the UMS3 system. All connections were matched to 50 Ω characteristic impedance.

The input electric power P e in the acoustic field measurements was kept at levels below 50 mW to satisfy the quasi-linear measurement conditions as per IEC 62556 standard, where the local distortion parameter, σ q, is less than 0.5. De-ionized and de-gassed water was used in measurements. The de-gassing was performed prior to each measurement session with target oxygen level below 2 mg/l, ensuring that throughout the entire measurement session lasting up to 3 h, the oxygen level was below 4 mg/l.

Acoustic field measurements were performed first by finding the spatial peak value indicating a location very close to focal point. Then axial and lateral pressure profile scans were performed with a step size of 10 μm with origin at maximum pressure point. Similarly, pressure plane scans in the XY plane (plane perpendicular to acoustic axis with origin in maximum pressure point) were caried out with a step size of 10 μm (both directions). Scans in the XZ plane (plane parallel to acoustic axis with origin in maximum pressure spatial point) were carried out with step sizes of 10 μm (X-direction) and 25 μm (Z-direction). Figure 3 depicts the setup and indicates the system of coordinates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of temperature in tissue-mimicking phantom
ce:section.ce:para: Measurement of transient temperature in the focal zone was performed using the smallest commercially available micro-thermocouple probe to accommodate the extremely small dimensions of the focal zone, as well as for fast temperature rise. A Type T probe (IT-24P, Physitemp Instruments, Clifton, NJ, USA) was used. The thermocouple had a total diameter (including insulation) of 130 μm and was insulated using polyurethane coating. During measurements, the thermocouple was inserted into the TMP from the bottom of the sample holder, so the thermocouple active junction was in the center of the focal zone (Fig. 4 ). The sample holder was specifically designed for the purpose and was printed from polylactic acid filament using a 3-D printer. The setup allowed for XYZ manipulation of the TMP with respect to the ultrasound field generated by the transducer. The thermocouple holder was additionally equipped with a mechanism allowing for precise movement in the Z-direction along main acoustic axis.

A custom-built thermocouple-to-voltage transmitter was constructed using the XTR101AP precision, low-drift, signal conditioner (Texas Instruments, Dallas, TX, USA). The transmitter's circuitry was designed for high-speed response using optimized configuration of filtering capacitors. Additionally, a p–n junction–based circuit was used for cold junction compensation. The transmitter was terminated using a 250 Ω resistor (0.1 Ω tolerance) looking into 1 MΩ input impedance of the digital oscilloscope. The transmitter was calibrated using a standardized thermocouple type T reference table.

The end-to-end time constant of the thermocouple/transmitter system, defined as the time to attain 67% of the final reading level in response to step temperature rise, was measured by fast immersion of the thermocouple into a tank filled with warm water at approximately 35°C (ambient temperature equal to 23°C). The time constant was determined on the basis of five independent immersions, and was equal to 14.5 ms with an 0.8 ms standard deviation.
The acquisition of the output voltage in response to temperature rise was acquired using an oscilloscope (DSOX3024T, Keysight Technologies). The temperature was sampled at a rate equal to 1 kHz.

In experiments with TMPs, the acoustic power delivery was controlled entirely by a commercially available medical HIFU system (System ONE-M TOOsonix A/S, Hørsholm, Denmark). The system is essentially a radiofrequency (RF) power source with built-in capability to regulate the frequency and power level of the signal. Additionally, the RF signal is generated in the form of bursts with precisely pre-defined duration, yielding full control over the total energy of the burst, being the product of power and burst duration. The system is also handling individual electro-acoustic efficiency (EAE) calibration curves for each transducer together with its unique resonance frequency, allowing therefore direct control of the acoustic power being produced by the transducers. The EAE curves of HIFU transducers were obtained in a wide acoustic power range from 0.5 W to around 10 W using a radiation force balance (RFB, Precision Acoustics, Dorset, UK), with the acoustic target calibrated up to 20 MHz. The system can control burst duration with a resolution of 1 ms, in the range 50–500 ms.

As the selected modeling methodology allowed only for calculation of thermal effects, the acoustic power level during temperature measurements in the TMP was kept low (≤2.6 W) to minimize the impact of non-thermal effects (e.g., cavitation). Moreover, the irreversible chemical and structural changes of the TMP, such as protein denaturation, were minimized at those power levels, allowing several experiments in one location of TMP with very high reproducibility of results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical model of acoustic field in lossless medium
ce:section.ce:para:
Initially the acoustic field profiles were modeled using an approximation by (Lucas and Muir 1982), where the acoustic field generated by a spherical radiator is derived by transforming the problem of radiation from the concave spherical source into a problem of radiation from a phase-modulated plane source placed in an infinite compliant baffle at the origin of the main axis at z = 0. In comparison to the well-known O'Neil (1949) model, this model requires only one integration step, and works under similar assumptions given by

Separate analytical models of a spherical radiator of aperture a with central hole of aperture a 1 (see Fig. 5) have been published (e.g., Beissner 2012); however, it was found that pressure distribution of a transducer with a central hole can be well modeled by subtracting the pressure field of the spherical radiator of aperture a 1 from the field of a spherical radiator of aperture a defined by eqns (5) to (8). Later in the text, the model given by these equations is referred to as the Lucas–Muir model.

Focal strength in the context of focusing ultrasound was studied by Kossoff (1979) and further elaborated in, for example, Goldstein (2006). It is basically a relation of the focal length F of a focusing source to transition distance z T of the equivalent flat source of the same aperture a operating at the same frequency f. .

Table 1 contains parameters of the piezoelectric components used in fabrication of the transducers, where only the frequency is directly measured and can be used in subsequent modeling. Other parameters such as outer diameter (OD) and inner diameter (ID), as well as the radius of curvature (RoC), are nominal as they are subject to tolerances of the manufacturing process. RoC is well maintained in practice with some ±100 μm tolerance; therefore, the nominal value of 15 mm is used in modeling. OD and ID, however, deserve more consideration as there are a number of factors with impact on the effective values. Here, effective OD and ID are understood as parameters directly affecting the resulting acoustic field.

The OD and ID of the ceramic component itself (excluding electrodes) are in practice well maintained with very tight tolerances (±50 μm); however, the actual effective OD and ID are defined by the electroded area of the piezoelectric component and its boundary conditions. The electrodes of the piezoelectric components were fabricated using a pad printing technique, which may lead to relatively large variability of the effective coverage of the ceramic part, especially in areas close to the inner and outer edges of the bowl. This, combined with a semi-rigid boundary condition, caused by epoxy bonding of the component to the housing, leads to the conclusion that the effective outer diameter is smaller and the effective inner diameter is larger than its nominal dimensions.

Effective diameters of the transducer were numerically identified using measurements of pressure amplitude p m(0, zi ) along the main acoustic axis in discrete locations zi , where i = 1, 2, …, N. N is the number of measurements around the focal point in a quasi-linear regime. Equations (5) and (6) define analytically the same pressure distribution as a function of parameters of the acoustic source. Identification of the effective parameters can therefore be expressed as minimalization of the goal function as follows:
(13)

It is worth emphasizing that the parameters OD, ID and RoC refer to a physical transducer, while aperture a, aperture of the hole a 1 and focal radius F are analogous parameters of the acoustic source of the model.

In the results presented, minimization per eqn (13) was performed using function fmincon from MATLAB Optimization Toolbox version 9.2 (The MathWorks, Natick, MA, USA). The function performs a minimum search of a non-linear multivariable goal function with constraints.
The search space was defined by the set of parameters a, a 1 around their nominal values. Interestingly, the minimization (13) was also used to identify the pressure at the surface of the source p 0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Numerical modeling of acoustic and temperature fields in lossy media
ce:section.ce:para:
Additionally, modeling of acoustic field generated by strongly focused transducers at high power levels was performed using the wide-angle Khokhlov–Zabolotskaya–Kuznetsov (WAKZK) method (Hamilton and Blackstock 1997; Zhou 2015), where the high-order parabolic axisymmetric Westervelt equation is integrated into the frequency domain. The calculations were performed using a public domain HITU Simulator (Soneson 2009), which is available in the form of an explicit MATLAB code through the United States Food and Drug Administration website. HITU Simulator in version 2.0 allows for modeling of wide-angle focused transducers, that is, transducers of low f-number. The calculations were performed using MATLAB R2021b (The MathWorks, Natick, MA, USA). HITU Simulator also provides a code for calculation of temperature fields caused by acoustic attenuation of acoustic waves in media. The temporal average acoustic intensity and heat power density are calculated based on a temporal pressure field. Then the final temporal temperature field is calculated by integration of the bio-heat transfer equation (BHT) in response to power density distribution. The model assumes power-law attenuation in layered media, as
(14)
Re
[
α
( f )
]
=
α 0
(
f /
f 0
)
η
where α is frequency-dependent attenuation, α 0 is attenuation at f 0 = 1 MHz and η is the exponent parameter of attenuation. More details are provided in Soneson (2019).

First, HITU Simulator was used to model acoustic pressure in water generated by transducers T04A, T07A and T20A at the exact acoustic power levels (quasi-linear regime) used in the acoustic tank measurements to validate the numerical model that was further used in modeling at higher power levels. Effective values of the ID and OD identified using the analytical model and eqn (13) were used in calculations.

Next, a two-layer model of the interaction of the acoustic field from transducer T20B coupled through water into a TMP was built to further explore the thermal effects of a strongly focused field in the TMP gels. The geometry of the computational domain, as well as the parameters of the transducer, are illustrated in Figure 6 .

In all simulations the number of computational nodes per wavelength of the highest harmonic was 15 in the radial direction and 10 in the axial direction. The number of harmonics was set to 128. The thermal calculations were performed on a computational domain with exactly the same dimensions, however with the number of nodes reduced by a factor of 3 in the axial direction and a factor of 2 in the radial direction. The latter was chosen as the temperature field because its variability is lower and it does not require the same dense computational grid as the pressure field does.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Numerical identification of acoustic and thermal parameters of TMPs
ce:section.ce:para: Selected parameters of the TPM material, namely, specific heat capacity C p, attenuation exponent η, thermal conductivity κ, as well as the fraction of heating caused by attenuation ϑ, were estimated from measurements of transient temperature in the focal point.

Because of the finite size of the temperature probe comparable to the size of the focal zone, the estimation procedure included an additional step of calculation of the spatial temperature average around the spatial maximum temperature at every step of the process. The spatial temperature average was calculated by averaging temperature enclosed within a sphere with pre-defined diameter equal to 100 μm, which corresponded to the diameter of the sensing element of the thermocouple used in transient temperature measurements. One needs to notice that the total diameter of the temperature probe (including insulator) was about 130 μm; therefore, 100 μm was used, assuming reasonable thickness of the insulation of 15 μm.

The final minimization task leading to estimation of the selected parameters can be expressed as
(15)
min
C p

∑

where T m(t i) is temperature in focal point measured at time t = ti , where i = 1, 2, …, N, N is the number of temporal measurements and
T
WAKZK
A
(
t i
)
is the spatial average calculated for a sphere of diameter 100 μm around spatial temperature maximum for the temperature field modeled by WAKZK followed by BHT. It is worth noting that the minimization as described by eqn (15) depends on a relatively large number of parameters; however, those were assumed to be known and well defined and are listed in Table 4. To account for differences in time stepping between measurements and WAKZK simulation, the spatial average of temperature according to WAKZK was piecewise-linearly interpolated before calculating goal function as per (15).
The estimation procedure was performed for temporal temperature measurements in the focal point with transducer T20B at input acoustic power equal to 0.5 W and a burst duration of 500 ms.

In the results presented, minimization as per equation (15) was performed using the function fmincon. The search space was defined by the set of TMP material parameters C p, η and κ around their nominal values found in the literature (Divkovic and Jenne 2005; Lafon et al. 2005). Parameter ϑ was initially put at 75% with a natural limit of 100%.

The optimization procedure involved a significant number of evaluations of the goal function, which in practice meant a full run of WAKZK followed by BHT. Each run took 1 h at 128 harmonics on a decent personal computer with a modern multicore processor. Therefore, the optimization was performed in two steps: first, using a reduced number of harmonics (32) and then again using parameters found in the first step as starting point with an increased number of harmonics (128).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements and modeling of acoustic field in quasi-linear conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Table 2 summarizes basic parameters as well as measurements obtained from T04A, T07A and T20A transducers operating at around 4, 7 and 20 MHz, respectively. Because of the quasi-linear conditions, the peak negative and peak positive pressures were similar (within 10%); therefore, only average pressure peak p pk values are given. The EAE values were estimated from pressure amplitudes measured using the acoustic tank. These values are comparable to EAE values measured for the same transducers using the RFB (Precision Acoustics, Dorset, UK) at low power levels (P a <1 W). Typically, the EAE values drop with higher power levels because of increased loss both in the matching circuits and in the piezoelectric component itself.

Figure 7 depicts the lateral and axial pressure profiles at the focal point generated by transducers T04A, T07A and T20A at around 4, 7 and 20 MHz, respectively. Figure 7 also illustrates the basic properties of the focal zone expressed by the DoF as well as FD (Angelsen et al. 1995; Kino 1987; Maréchal et al. 2007). The depth of focus, sometimes also called the depth of field (Szabo 2013) or focal depth, is defined as

A drop of 3, 6 or 12 dB can be used (Kino 1987; Gohari 1997) as depicted in Figure 7; however, 6 dB seems to be the best choice in the case of HIFU transducers, as its measurement is not affected by pre-lobes or side lobes, as is the case for 12 dB. Moreover, a 6 dB drop represents a reduction of pressure amplitude by 50% or reduction of magnitude of acoustic intensity by 75% compared with the maximum value in the proximity of the focus, which represents well where most of the energy is deposited during HIFU sonification.

Table 3 summarizes the values of effective parameters of the transducers identified by fitting the axial amplitude distribution described by eqns (5) and (6) into measurements performed with the acoustic tank and fiberoptic hydrophone. Based on the listed values, we can estimate the effective aperture half-angle given by

where a can be substituted by the estimated effective OD of the transducer, assuming that RoC = F = 15 mm. The effective product of wavenumber and aperture
k a
is calculated in a similar way, taking into account the effective value of OD and the wavenumber calculated at the actual operating frequency of the transducer.

The assumption of the Lucas–Muir model, eqn (1), basically means that the half-angle of the aperture
ζ 0
≪
45 ∘
, which in fact is not the case, as outlined in Table 3. This requirement is very strict but, as argued in Coulouvrat (1993) and further elaborated on in Goldstein (2006), the contribution of secondary diffraction caused by the transducer curvature is limited when ζ 0 ≤45°, as in the devices studied. Moreover, the requirement, eqn (2), is fulfilled as well (see Table 3), which explains why the employed model works well under the given conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of temperature in focal zone
ce:section.ce:para: Figure 8 illustrates the results of temperature measurements in TMP gel in response to a burst of acoustic signal from transducer T20B in the location of the highest temperature rise at different power levels and different burst durations. The measurements were performed in an air-conditioned environment with a constant ambient temperature of 23°C.

The error shading depicted in Figure 8 reflects the measurement error defined as 3.9% of reading + 1°C; 3.9% is three times the standard deviation estimated from measurements performed under the same sonication conditions in different locations of the TMP when the procedure of finding maximum temperature was repeated every time a new location was selected. This process was found to be the primary reason for measurement error. The locations of sonication were chosen far from the edges of the TMP gel sample, so that the acoustic and thermal boundary conditions were comparable and stable in all measurements.

Additionally, Figure 8 illustrates the results of simulations using HITU Simulator 2.0. Acoustic as well as thermal parameters of materials used in the calculations are listed in Table 4 . The estimated parameters are marked. The parameter estimation was performed using the temperature curve in Figure 8B for the case 0.5 W.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Numerical modeling of acoustic and temperature field
ce:section.ce:para:
A selected case of transducer T20B operating at 20.186 MHz generating an acoustic burst of 500 ms at P a = 0.5 W coupled through water into the TMP gel was further studied in detail using numerical modeling. Figure 9 (A, B) illustrates the distribution of peak negative pressure p – and peak positive pressure p +, respectively. Additionally, the magnitude of acoustic intensity
I ^
and heat density Q distribution around the focal point are illustrated in Figure 9 (C, D). The border between the coupling water and the TMP gel is clearly marked. A high concentration of energy expressed by high levels of intensity and heat density is noticeable around the designed focal point within the volume of the tissue-mimicking phantom.

Development of spatial peak temperature as well as spatial average around spatial peak temperature are illustrated in Figure 10 A. Because of the large spatial temperature gradient, there is a significant difference between the two, despite a relatively small averaging spherical volume of diameter 100 μm. The direct comparison between the size of averaging and the temperature distribution around the focal point is illustrated in Figure 10B, where the final temperature field at t = 500 ms is given. A characteristic triangular temperature distribution is clearly visible.

The development of the temperature field caused by the interaction of the acoustic wave with medium is further illustrated in Figure 11 , which contains contour maps at selected time points. The characteristic triangular shape of the field becomes gradually visible toward the end of the burst duration.

An integrated microscopic camera was used to image the surface of an object aligned with the end of the transducer head at the optical focus, thus allowing real-time and simultaneous observation of the operation area during HIFU action. In Figure 13 (A, B) are test images of a Microscope Micrometer Calibration Ruler (Dino-Lite, AnMo Electronics Corp., New Taipei City, Taiwan) as observed through transducer T20B. The image has a good-quality imaging area about 8 mm in diameter with a relatively small fringing effect (Fig. 13A). Additionally, the good resolution is confirmed by the well-defined test circle of 100 μm in Figure 13B. Figure 13C is an image of human skin taken through the transducer. It reveals details of the skin surface and hairs, as well as the characteristic triangular pattern, with very good clarity and detail. Moreover, half of the image is a Miescher nevus with all its characteristics. The image is comparable to industry-standard dermoscopic instruments, designed for diagnostic purposes only.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size and shape of the focal zone
ce:section.ce:para:
For a given transducer, depth of focus can be estimated using following formulas (Gohari 1997; Maréchal et al. 2007):
(21)
Do

X is a coefficient, where X indicates the pressure drop in X dB by which the focal zone is defined. Similarly, focal diameter can be expressed as
(22)

where c FD
X is a coefficient, and X indicates the pressure drop in decibels by which focal diameter is defined. This means that both characteristic dimensions of the focal zone are determined by: the speed of sound in the given medium, frequency of the wave as well as the design of the source transducer through the F #. It is therefore seen that to achieve a small focal zone, one needs to use low-F # devices as well as high frequency.

Coefficients c DOF and c FD can be numerically estimated using formulas (21) and (22). Averaged data over the studied F # range are given in Table 5 . Similar values of selected coefficients have been published in Gohari (1997) and Maréchal et al. (2007) and remain in agreement with the presented data within a few percent of error.

Furthermore, it seems that at 20 MHz, the values of coefficients remain reasonably constant over a larger range of values of F # as depicted in Figure 14 . The same also indicates how the maximum pressure point, z 0, shifts toward the source. Moreover, it also illustrates the ratio of maximum pressure to pressure at the geometrical focal point. Both depend on F # of the transducer, as the lower the F #, the closer is the maximum pressure point to the geometrical focal point and the closer is the maximum pressure to pressure in the geometrical focal point. It can be mentioned that for sources with large apertures operating at high frequency, for example, 20 MHz, the maximum pressure point is located almost in the geometrical focal point, and in practice they can be used interchangeably.

All focal zone indicators based on different measures of pressure drop in decibels can be used in characterizing the high-intensity focused ultrasound zone; for instance, Kino (1987) uses 3 dB, and Maréchal et al. (2007) use both 3 and 6 dB. However, 6 dB seems the most useful as it indicates a 50% pressure drop in comparison to the maximum pressure in the center of the focus, which is easy to measure and quantify. The 6 dB drop also indicates the 75% drop in magnitude of acoustic intensity, which gives a good indication of where most of the energy is concentrated. In clinical terms, the 6 dB focal zone is therefore a good measure of where most of the clinical effect originates. The thinner the tissue to be treated, the smaller the focal zone should be. Of course, the final clinical effect will be determined by a number of other factors, total energy deposited being one of the most important.

A broad range of sizes of focal zones are attainable for a typical frequency range used in HIFU combined with typical f-numbers, as depicted in Figure 15 . This article is extending the range by adding an extremely low f-number as well as a high-frequency range, making HIFU appropriate for applications in precise treatment of human skin.

The theoretical predictions defined by eqns (21) and (22) can be compared with measurements of focal zone dimensions given in Figure 7. Table 6 summarizes the data obtained for three different strongly focused transducers operating at significantly different frequencies. In general, very good agreement between the theoretical predictions and measured values is obtained. The best fit is seen in the estimation of the focal diameter. This is because of the lower impact of the central hole, as the predictions based on formulas (21) and (22) do not take into account the central hole and its impact on pressure distribution. This will be discussed further in the following sections. Another important aspect to mention is that the measurement at the 12 dB drop is also affected by the side or front lobes in pressure distribution, especially at lower frequency. This is yet another argument for using the 6 dB measure as it is easier to implement in practice and less vulnerable to error.

It is important to emphasize that the focal zone in water generated by a transducer working at around 20 MHz is appropriate for skin treatment as it provides the necessary precision for operating across the depth of the skin, as the attainable depth of focus is in the range of 500 μm, enabling the treatment of a chosen layer of the skin without affecting other layers. For example, it makes it possible to operate only in the epidermis and basement membrane layer of the skin with only a small impact on dermis.

In the literature, the shape of the focal zone is often characterized as cigar-shaped or ellipsoidal. From eqns (21) and (22), one can deduce the dimensions along the axial and radial directions. Interestingly, because of the nature of the field, it is impossible to obtain a 6 dB focal zone in the shape of a sphere as it would require F # ≈ 0.146, whereas F # = 0.5 represents a hemisphere.

Figure 16 depicts the shape of a 6 dB focal zone reconstructed from planar measurements of the pressure field using fiberoptic hydrophone generated by transducer T20A at P a = 20 mW. The reconstructed focal zone is elongated over the axial distance and characterized by the DoF and FD as predicted by eqns (21) and (22).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Focus strength and focusing gain under quasi-linear conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Strong-focus critical values of F # are given in Table 7 . For all transducers listed, F # = 0.75, which is significantly smaller than critical values at each analyzed frequency, meaning that all transducers are, in fact, strongly focused.

Given that focusing gain expressed by eqn (7) is entirely dependent on the geometry of the transducer and operating frequency, and assuming that losses in a medium can be neglected, the focusing gain can be calculated based on the measurements in Table 2 combined with estimates of p 0 from Table 3. In such a case, the measured focusing gain is a ratio defined as
(23)
G m
=
p pk
p 0

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of the central hole on pressure distribution
ce:section.ce:para: Many of the tools described, such as expressions (21) and (22), silently assume a spherical aperture, a, is the source of ultrasound. In this article, however, the experimentally studied transducer also has a central hole to provide optical access for real-time observation of the treated area. It is interesting to study how axial and radial pressures are affected by the size of the central hole defined by its radius, a 1. According to Figure 17 A, the larger the central hole, the wider is the main beam along the axial distance, and hence the larger is the DoF. However, the amplitude of pre- and post-lobes seems not to be affected, only their location. Contrary to that, the width of the main beam along radial distance is maintained even for a relatively large central hole; however, the amplitude of the side lobes increases significantly. Pressure in Figure 17 is normalized to the maximum value for each case separately for clarity. However, if it was normalized to maximum pressure attainable for a source without a central hole then it would indicate that peak pressure values would be lower for higher diameters of the central hole at constant p 0. This is simply due to the fact that the larger the central hole, the lower is the effective radiating surface of the source.

The shape of the lesions depicted in Figure 12 can be explained by the temperature distribution around the focal zone depicted in Figures 10B and 11. The linear bio-heating simulation indicates development of the characteristic triangular shape of the lesion in response to a strongly focused HIFU field. Moreover, the spatial development of the lesion is directed toward the surface of the phantom, and its depth is pre-determined by the NPD for the given design of transducer.

The lesion shape in Figure 12C can be directly linked with the results presented in the numerical modeling section as a transducer with the same NPD (2.3 mm) was used at low power levels, P a ≤ 0.7 W, for thermal measurements in TMPs per Figure 8 and imaging of thermal lesions in TMPs at higher power, P a = 2.6 W, per Figure 12C. However, it is beyond the limits of the linear bio-heating model to predict the temperature field distribution at P a = 2.6 W, as the acoustic field tends to be highly affected by formation of the shock wave.

Figure 18 illustrates the temporal pressure in the proximity of the focal point at the acoustic power levels studied. Results at P a ≤ 0.7 W indicate that the volumetric heat source distribution in the medium can be estimated using the linear bio-heating model, as pressure waveforms do not contain a lot of higher-order harmonics. At P a = 2.6 W, it is however very clear that a shock wave can be formed. In such cases, the heat rate is proportional to the third power of amplitude of the shock wave, which is not considered in the numerical model employed. Moreover, the high-power case is additionally affected by numerous mechanical effects such as cavitation and streaming that require further studies to account for the energy balance between the input acoustic power and the recorded results in the form of an observable lesion in the TMP.

Small dimensions of the focal zone expressed by depth of focus as well as focal diameter are the prerequisite for precise ultrasonic therapy of an organ having a very shallow structure, such as human skin. Interestingly, most of the HIFU research has gone into enlarging the focal zones with the aim of increasing the efficiency of the treatment. This is logically justified as most of the interest has gone into treatment of relatively large organs and pathologies located deep in the human body.

Depth of focus (6 dB) of some 400–500 μm is desired for treatment of human skin. The current study determined that this calls for use of frequencies around 20 MHz and higher, as even strongly focused transducers operating at 7 MHz produce depths of focus of some 1200 μm. In such cases, it is very likely that HIFU would reach into deep dermis and below, possibly damaging deep structures below the skin. Depth of focus and focal diameter are defined by the F # of the transducer and operating frequency. Operation at 20 MHz is, however, in practice a more challenging task as manufacture of a large aperture and extremely thin piezoelectric components might be impossible. Therefore, an operation at the third harmonic is presented in this study as the desired solution.

Modeling of the acoustic field of strongly focused transducers working at 20 MHz using the Lucas–Muir model yields surprisingly good results despite the extremely low f-number of the devices and extending beyond one of the assumptions of the model. Moreover, this model is a good tool when combined with measurements providing additional validation of the modeled acoustic fields. The effective dimensions of the transducers as estimated by fitting the model with measurement data confirm that in practice the outer diameter of the device is smaller while the inner diameter is larger compared with the nominal values. This is consistent with the expectations as the impact of the pad printing of electrodes and boundary conditions is significant.

Operation at 20 MHz combined with strong focus produces large focal gain in the order of hundreds, confirmed by the estimated data based on measurements as well as the analytical formula. Interestingly, the values based on measurements are around 10% lower compared with the theoretical values, which would correspond to the impact of losses in water neglected in the theoretical model. High focusing gain can be desired when it comes to the miniaturization of the whole device, as it would lead to a need for a significantly lower-power driver, which in practice reduces the cost and makes the device more accessible. There is, however, a limit defined by the ratio of aperture to wavelength, which one would like to keep large to ensure strong focusing.

The optical observation of the skin through the coupling medium is an important element of a device for ultrasonic therapy of the most superficial layers of the human skin. This is enabled by the fact that HIFU operates very close to the surface of the skin, where conventional ultrasound imaging is less relevant. Moreover, in the case of treatment of dermis or epidermis, most of the guidance for operators comes from the features located on the surface of the skin rather than the ultrasonic scan being perpendicular to the surface of the skin. The hole in the ultrasonic transducer is the critical element of the optical observation system. It has, however, been found that if the central hole is small (say <25%) in comparison to the aperture of the transducers, its impact is limited. This manifests in only a small increase in depth of focus and increased amplitude of side lobes in comparison to a comparable solid transducer with the same aperture and radius of curvature.

As indicated by the measurements of the temperature increase in the focal point of an acoustic wave at 20 MHz located beneath the surface of the TMP, the peak temperature is well controlled by the acoustic power level and is linear up to some 0.7 W. At this point, the peak temperature reaches 100°C, beyond which there are several additional effects, such as boiling, increased cavitation, and so on. It must, however, be pointed out that all such effects are confined to an extremely small volume because of the very small size of the focal zone. Even going to high power levels of 2.6 W produces well-defined small lesions, as indicated by the measurements in the TMP gels. Moreover, the nominal penetration depth yields excellent control where the energy is deposited. It must also be pointed out that the dynamics of the temperature field indicates that the lesion “grows” during sonification toward the surface of the sample. During this growth, the lesions gradually form a characteristic triangular shape, with the “tip” of the lesion located at the level defined by penetration depth. This is an important intrinsic element of patient safety as the deeper layers of tissue thereby remain unaffected by the HIFU.

Although an estimation of the material properties of TMP gel was not the central topic of this article, the procedure described seems to work well comparing the estimated values with the values published previously for similar compositions (Lafon et al. 2001, 2005; Howard et al. 2003; Divkovic and Jenne 2005). It is worth mentioning that a number of parameters can be estimated based on a single temporal measurement of temperature. Among the estimated parameters, the exponent of attenuation η is important as the estimation was performed at relatively high frequency, especially for HIFU. It is, however, important to mention that parameter η was estimated with the assumption α 0 = 33 dB/m at 1 MHz (Divkovic and Jenne 2005). A natural extension of the experiment would be to generate data at lower frequencies, for example, 4 and 7 MHz, and perform estimation as per eqn (15); however, this was beyond the scope of this work.

Characterization of HIFU devices above 20 MHz (Durando et al. 2015) poses a number of challenges mainly because of wavelength in water in the range of few tens of micrometers. This has a direct impact on the size of the focal zone and, therefore, requirements regarding diameter of the active area of the hydrophone, its bandwidth and the stepping resolution during acoustic tank measurements. The measurement equipment was used at its limits, even though truly state-of-the-art equipment was employed. For transducers operating above 25 MHz, one would need to use a stepping resolution of some 1–5 μm, a bandwidth >50 MHz and a hydrophone capable of handling higher acoustic intensities with an active element diameter of some 1–5 μm.

The measurements of temperature in the center of the focal zone indicate that application of the linear bio-heating model is limited to acoustic power levels below 0.7 W for the given configuration. This is why the temperature field simulations were performed at 0.5 W (500 ms) in comparison to experiments with lesioning in TMP gels performed at 2.6 W (500 ms). It is therefore argued that a full understanding of the effects of strongly focused HIFU would require implementation of shock wave heating, as well as consideration of the energy loss caused by mechanical effects and change of phase. It can also be concluded that an interesting “side effect” of the parameter estimation based on WAKZK and linear BHT modes is the estimation of the fraction of heating caused by attenuation. This fraction was estimated to be 83%, which can be interpreted as indication that up to peak temperatures below 100°C, only 17% of the acoustic energy is dissipated non-thermally. The extension of the models for strongly focused transducers with emphasis on shock wave heating is concluded to be the natural suggestion for researchers working with numerical modeling of high-power HIFU in the future.

The presented devices are equipped with an optical observation system allowing simultaneous treatment and observation of the skin surface. In comparison to conventional cosmetic applications of HIFU where imaging cannot be performed at the same time as the therapy, the analyzed devices are concluded to be more appropriate for treatment of precisely selected and confined layers of the human skin.
